questions.question: What is the goal of a lightning capacity round?
options: Find the smallest set of estimates that changes the architecture or reveals the bottleneck.
Produce an exact spreadsheet for every subsystem.
Avoid making assumptions under uncertainty.
Delay design discussion until perfect math is complete.
questions.explanation: The round should create design signal quickly, not exhaustive arithmetic.

questions.question: Why is average QPS alone often misleading?
options: Because burst factors, skew, or amplification can dominate the real capacity risk.
Because averages are never useful in system design.
Because QPS only matters for batch systems.
Because latency has no relationship to load.
questions.explanation: Averages hide the exact effects that often break systems in production.

questions.question: What makes a lightning estimate high value in an interview?
options: It leads to one concrete design implication, such as a partitioning, caching, or backlog decision.
It uses more decimal places than the interviewer expects.
It covers every possible resource equally.
It avoids uncertainty ranges.

questions.question: Which metric best tracks whether lightning rounds are improving your practice quality?
options: The percentage of rounds that name a bottleneck and one resulting architecture consequence inside the time limit.
Only the total number of formulas used.
The number of diagrams drawn per round.
The amount of storage examples memorized.
questions.explanation: Speed matters only if the round still reaches a useful conclusion.

questions.question: Why should a round include one amplification estimate when relevant?
options: Because fanout, retries, replication, or invalidation often multiply the true load far beyond raw request count.
Because amplification matters only in social networks.
Because amplification replaces the need for latency targets.
Because amplification estimates make storage growth irrelevant.
questions.explanation: Multipliers often dominate the real system cost and risk.

questions.question: What is the trade-off of range-based estimates instead of exact numbers?
options: They are more honest and adaptable, but can feel less tidy than precise-looking arithmetic.
They make the architecture impossible to reason about.
They remove the need for assumptions.
They are useful only for storage systems.
questions.explanation: Rough ranges are often more credible than fake precision under interview uncertainty.

questions.question: If a prompt suddenly introduces a 20x peak factor, what should change first?
options: Revisit admission control, backlog behavior, and burst headroom rather than relying on the old steady-state model.
Only the database schema names.
The observability section should be removed to save time.
Nothing important, because peak factor rarely changes architecture.
questions.explanation: Burstiness often changes the correct architecture more than steady-state averages do.

questions.question: Why is it useful to stop after identifying one dominant bottleneck in a short round?
options: Because the point is to guide the design quickly, not to model every second-order effect before speaking.
Because systems have only one bottleneck forever.
Because follow-up questions will never test anything else.
Because bottlenecks matter only for storage-heavy prompts.
questions.explanation: A short round should optimize for clarity and momentum.
